steps.text: Flush the sampling device or system with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air or ultrapure nitrogen for a minimum of 24 hours before field deployment.
Verify that the analytical equipment used in performing this method meets the performance criteria in this section.
Demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation is non-biasing under zero-air.
Analyze humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air from a known clean source at the installation prior to initial use of the instrument.
Examine chromatograms for interferences and other chromatographic artifacts.
Take steps to remove the contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Repeat the analytical zero-air verification to ensure that you have mitigated any problems before using the analytical instrumentation.

Close the valve, remove the vacuum/pressure gauge, and loosely cap the canister using a cap fitting to ensure that leakage through the valve is accurately assessed while avoiding potential entry of debris into the valve during storage.
After a minimum of three days in storage, reinstall the vacuum/pressure gauge, open the valve, and record the canister pressure reading.

Determine the pressure decay rate as the absolute value of the difference between the initial and post-storage canister pressures. You must remove the canister from service if the pressure decay rate exceeds 0.69 kPa/storage day (0.2 in. Hg or 0.1 psia/storage day).

Pressurize the clean evacuated canister with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air. Do not use ultrapure nitrogen to pressurize the canister because the inert nitrogen atmosphere does not permit reactions within the canister that may occur under sampling conditions.
Allow the canister to equilibrate for a minimum of 24 hours.
After the equilibration period, conduct an initial cleanliness analysis as specified in Section 8.4 of this method.
Store the canister for a holding period equal to or exceeding the typical laboratory holding time, nominally 7 days from the canister fill date.
After the holding period, conduct a subsequent cleanliness analysis as specified in Section 8.5 of this method.

The results of both the initial and subsequent cleanliness analysis must meet the cleanliness criteria specified in Section 8.5 of this method to be used for sampling. You must reclean and retest canisters that fail the zero-air challenge.

Fill the clean evacuated canister with a humidified (40 to 50% RH) standard gas in HCF zero air with each target compound at approximately 100 to 500 pptv. Choose the selected challenge concentration based on the concentration expected to be measured during the sampling event.
Heat canisters by placing them in an enclosed oven during cleaning to facilitate removal of compounds.
Evacuate canisters to minimally 7 kPa absolute (28 in. Hg vacuum or 1 psia) and maintain this vacuum for at least 1 minute.
Pressurize canisters to 414 kPa absolute (≤ 30 psig) with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air and maintain this pressure for a minimum of 1 minute.
Repeat the cycle of canister evacuation and pressurization specified in Sections 8.4.4.1 and 8.4.4.2 of this method at least 5 times.
After cleaning, pressurize each canister from the batch with humidified HCF zero air and maintain that pressure for at least 24 hours.
Connect each canister to the analytical system and measure the concentration of each target compound according to the procedures in Section 11 of this method.
The canister background concentration for each target compound must be ≤ 20 pptv (0.02 ppbv) or undetected when a canister is filled to standard ambient pressure (101.3 kPa absolute or 14.7 psia).

Canisters that meet the blank criteria are suitable to be evacuated for use. If a canister fails to meet the criteria, you must not use that canister until it has been re-cleaned and has met the requirements in Section 8.5.3 of this method.
Prior to field deployment, evacuate canisters to ≤ 0.0067 kPa (≤ 0.002 in. Hg or 0.001 psia).
Follow the manufacturer’s instructions for cleaning components such as flow controllers and sampling unit parts, when necessary.
Flush the sampling units with humidified HCF zero air to remove contamination for at least 15 minutes.
Disassemble sampling components and visually inspect for cracks, abrasions, and residue prior to sonicating in deionized water for at least 30 minutes.
After flushing/sonication, rinse the components with clean deionized water and dry the components in an enclosed oven set to at least 50 °C for a minimum of 12 hours.
Following drying, reinspect components for defects, reassemble, and flush the sampler with humidified HCF zero air or ultrapure nitrogen for at least 12 hours.
Clean canisters and verify that the canisters meet cleanliness and vacuum criteria specified in Sections 8.3 through 8.5 of this method.

If canisters were previously cleaned and stored under pressure while awaiting use, you must evacuate the canisters prior to field deployment. If canisters were stored under vacuum, you must verify that the canisters continue to meet vacuum threshold requirements.
Clean and verify the cleanliness and flowrates of sample devices that you will use for sampling, and ensure that a clean particulate filter is placed in the inlet of the sampling device.
Establish sample codes (unique identifiers) and develop field data page and/or chain of custody (COC)/sample collection data form(s).

If shipping equipment into the field, make sure you have the proper number of canisters and sampling devices for the number of samples required for the sampling location and QC samples, allowing for sufficient timing for samples to arrive at the site.

Develop a unique sampling location ID. The sampling location must meet any requirements set in the applicable regulation and be in a secure location that protects the canister and sampling inlet from unwanted tampering or damage.
Prepare and secure the canisters for transport.
Ship canisters in protective hard-shell boxes and/or sturdy cardboard boxes to ensure canister longevity.
Do not use boxes that have lost integrity or rigidity.

Prepare at least seven blank samples according to the procedures Section 9.3.2 of this method using sampling media (i.e., canisters) that have been deployed in the field, and cleaned per Section 8.4 of this method.
The blank samples must be prepared in at least three batches on three separate calendar dates and analyzed on three separate calendar dates according to the procedures in Section 11 of this method.

Prepare at least seven spike samples according to the procedures in either Section 10.2 or 10.3 of this method, at a concentration of the target compound within a factor of five of the expected detection limits.
The spike samples must be prepared in at least three batches on three separate calendar dates and analyzed on three separate calendar dates according to the procedures in Section 11 of this method.
Compute the standard deviation for the replicate blank samples concentrations and multiply this value by 3.14 to determine the blank MDL (MDLb).
Compute the standard deviation for the replicate spike sample concentrations and multiply this value by 3.14 to determine the spike MDL (MDLs).
Select the greater of MDLb or MDLs as the MDL for the compound of interest.
If the MDL does not meet the concentration requirement, perform corrective action and repeat the MDL determination.
MDL determinations must be repeated at least annually or whenever significant changes have been made to the sampling or analytical system.
Analyze the CB when the ICAL is established and when preparing any new CCV standard.
Analyze the field duplicate during the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field sample.
Analyze at least one replicate analysis for each set of field-collected samples.
Take steps to remove contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Check for cold spots in transfer line, gas impurities, sorbent traps, GC column, system leaks, and/or poor moisture management.
Consult instrument manufacturer for steps to eliminate bias, as necessary.
Zero-air challenge of autosamplers associated with analytical instrument systems.
After establishing the ICAL, each port of the autosampler is tested to demonstrate cleanliness (positive bias) by analyzing humidified zero air.
Perform a known-standard challenge of autosamplers associated with analytical instrument systems.
After establishing the ICAL, each port of the autosampler is tested with a reference standard to demonstrate that the autosampler is not causing bias.
Canister leak check verification that canisters are leak-free by performing a pressure decay test of a canister pressurized to approximately 203 kPa absolute.
Remove canister from service.
Repair canister connections and/or valve.
Clean and retest canisters that fail the zero-air verification.
Remove canisters from service that cannot pass the zero-air verification after the cleaning process.
Clean and retest canisters that fail the known-standard verification.
Remove canisters from service that cannot pass known-standard verification after the cleaning process.
Take steps to assess positive bias of sampling system by collecting humidified zero air through the sampling device/system.
Repeat analysis.
Reprepare the MB canister and reanalyze.
Check the system for leaks.
Reanalyze CB.
Reprepare CB and ICAL canister standards.
Check for preconcentrator volume measurement error.
Reanalyze primary sample and collocated duplicate.
Flag data for possible invalidation.
Prior to initial use and upon replacement of transfer lines.
Prior to initial use and annually thereafter.
Prior to initial field deployment and every six months thereafter, following maintenance (component replacement), or after collection of potentially contaminating samples.

Prior to initial field deployment and at least every six months thereafter, following maintenance (component replacement), or after collection of potentially contaminating samples or damaging sample matrices that may impact the activity of the flow path surfaces.
Prior to ICAL and prior to each day’s analysis.

Before sample analysis, following failed BFB tune check (as applicable), failed IS criteria, or failed CCV criteria; annually, or when changes/maintenance to the instrument affect calibration response.
Prior to analyzing samples in an analytical sequence and at the end of a sequence, unless the sequence begins with an ICAL; recommended after every 10 sample injections.
Prior to ICAL and at the beginning of an analytical sequence.
Prior to and following the ICAL and daily following the IB/BFB and prior to the initial daily CCV/SSCV.
(1) Check for preconcentrator volume measurement error. (2) Reanalyze primary sample and collocated duplicate. (3) Flag data for possible invalidation.
Preconcentrator leak check Pressurize or evacuate the canister connection to verify as leak-free.
Each canister connected to the instrument prior to analysis.
< 3.4 kPa (0.5 psi) change per minute or as recommended by the manufacturer (see Section 11.4.2 of this method).
Check the tightness of all fittings and recheck.
Calculate the volume of water you must add to standard and blank canisters to achieve 40 to 50% RH at ambient laboratory temperature.
Use a bubbler or impinger within the dilution gas stream, add water to the canister, or use a combination of these two methods to add the calculated volume of deionized water to the canister.
Do not add water to the canister using a syringe via rubber septum or other materials that may introduce target or interfering compounds.
The gas dilution system must produce calibration gases whose measured concentration values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
The gas dilution system must be calibrated and verified per Section 6.10.1 of this method.
Prior to use, power on the dynamic dilution system and allow the diluent and stock gases to flow through the respective MFC at operating flow rates.
You must prepare humidified (40 to 50% RH) standards in canisters from low concentration to high concentration.
The final pressure of the calibration standard canister must not exceed the maximum pressure permitted by the preconcentrator.
The gas dilution system shall produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to an evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as you add gases.
Add stock and diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Measure the canister pressure before and after standard and diluent gases are bled into the canister, and input these pressures into the calculation of the dilution factor and final concentrations.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.
Condition preconcentrator traps when first installed to eliminate contaminants that act as interferences or chromatographic artifacts, per manufacturer recommendation.
Analyze the IBs and MBs to verify the preconcentrator system meets the method criteria.

Optimize GC conditions for compound separation and sensitivity by establishing GC carrier gas flow rates, oven temperature program, and instrument run time based on the manufacturer’s recommendations.

Tune/optimize the MS (quadrupole, ion trap, or TOF MS) to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications upon initial installation of the instrument and following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities.
Determine the peak areas.
Initiate the quantitation process using the software package of choice.
Calculate an instrument dilution correction factor (IDCF) if you analyzed an aliquot from the sample canister that is different from the typical analysis volume.
Use Equation 15 in Section 12 to determine the final concentration of each target compound in air.
Analyze samples using the same acquisition methods used for establishing calibration.
Verify each canister connection as leak-free through the preconcentrator.
Connect canisters to the autosampler or sample introduction lines and maintain the canister valves in the closed position.
Evacuate each port of the autosampler or sample introduction line and monitor for a change in pressure for 1 minute.
Implement corrective actions and retest if a sample line fails the leak check.
Evacuate all autosampler ports or sample introduction lines, open the canister valves, and document the leak check results.
Connect each sample canister to the preconcentration unit through a port and verify each canister as having a leak-free connection.
Accurately measure the sample aliquot volume for analysis.
Verify instrument calibration by analysis of a CCV before beginning sample analysis.

preconditions.text: If a canister continues to fail the zero-air challenges, remove the canister from service.
The RPD of the precision measurements should agree within ± 30% when both measurements are ≥ 5 times the MDL.
Before analyzing humidified zero air, establish the ICAL.
Before performing a known-standard challenge, establish the ICAL.

Allow gases to flow for at least the minimum time used during the yearly bias check in Section 6.10.1.3 of this method, to ensure the concentrations of the oHAPs in the blended gas are stable prior to transferring to the humidified canister.

When changing stock gas flow rate(s) to prepare a different concentration, allow the calibration gas sufficient time to flow through the system prior to preparation of the working calibration canister.
Before initiating the quantitation process, ensure that the peak areas have been determined.
If the aliquot analyzed is different from the typical analysis volume, calculate an IDCF before proceeding to step 4.
Prior to beginning an analytical sequence, verify each canister connection as leak-free.
Prior to each sample analysis sequence, connect each sample canister to the preconcentration unit.

constraints.text: Analysis must show that any detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample are at response levels that are expected to be < 20 pptv or not detected.

Verifies that all target compounds are detected by the system, that they respond consistently upon repeated injection, and that they exhibit sufficient response to be quantifiable at low concentrations.
Each target VOC's concentration must be < 20 pptv or preferably not detected.
Each target VOC's concentration must be within ± 15% of theoretical concentration.
A pressure change ≥ 0.69 kPa/day during canister leak check.
CB must be sufficiently clean such that little or no positive bias is imparted to the calibration.
Precision ≤ 30% RPD of target VOCs in the compared samples when both measurements are ≥ fivefold MDL.
Precision ≤ 25% RPD for target VOCs when both measurements are ≥ fivefold MDL.
